strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
2

Hunger is the key reason losing fat is difficult. If you weren't hungry, you could simply
consciously decide to eat less and fat loss would merely be a matter of time. In this
guide we'll give you tips to make your meal plan more satiating and conquer your
hunger.

All the scientific references and further details are provided in our online PT Course.
Ask your coach if you're interested in specific studies. This guide does not go into total
macronutrient intake, supplementation or meals consumed outside of your regular meal
plan.

3

1. Eat more food
By far the most common culprit of hunger is simply not eating enough food. Food's don't
differ that much in how satiating they are, but they do differ enormously in how many
calories they have. Your stomach roughly senses total pressure on the gut receptors,
not calorie intake, so one of the most effective ways to reduce your hunger is to reduce
the average calorie density of your diet. In terms of filling, low calorie foods, nothing
beats the following list.

1. Vegetables, including pumpkin, preferably dark (green) ones, excluding
potatoes and similar starches that are not commonly regarded as vegetables.
2. Strawberries, blackberries, grapefruit, papaya, chayote, starfruit, hog plums
and peaches (especially peaches on sugar-free syrup).
3. Vinegar, mustard, low calorie condiments, herbs and spices and anything with
zero calories.
4. Lean protein source, notably egg whites, chicken breast, white fish, tuna,
organ meat (kidney/liver), 1% beef, cottage cheese, quark and Greek yogurt.

4

2. Eat more fiber
Your coach should have programmed in enough dietary fiber in your diet. As a
guideline, women should consume over 25 grams of fiber per day and men should
consume over 38 grams per day. Those are the recommended daily intakes for
sedentary individuals, so you should think of these as bare minima as a strength
trainee. Don't skimp on your fiber intake and get it from whole foods. Supplements
aren't as effective and often upset your digestion.

5

3. Drink more calorie-free liquids
While liquids aren't as satiating as solid foods per unit of volume, many liquids are free
in terms of calories. Be sure to drink a lot, especially during and right before meals.
Liquid calories are extremely unsatiating per calorie, so stick with calorie-free liquids.
• Decaff teas and coffee. Tip: You can make extremely low-calorie cappuccino with
sucralose powder and just 25 ml of skimmed milk if you beat it thoroughly.
• Herbal teas.
• Water, preferably carbonated water. The gas puts extra pressure on the pressure
receptors in your gut, increasing your satiation.
• Diet sodas. There's nothing wrong with artificial sweeteners.

A useful trick to increase satiety is to drink a lot of carbonated water when you're
deciding whether to eat more or not. There's generally a certain point in a meal where
you're reasonably satiated, but you could eat more. Chugging a glass of carbonated
water at this point can be an effective 'finishing blow'.

6

4. Limit intra-meal variety
Variety is good, right? Not for your appetite. Your appetite is sensory-specific, which
means the more tastes you introduce in a meal, the larger your appetite. A good
template for most diets is to stick with a single high-quality protein source and one type
of filler food, usually a carbohydrate source, in each meal. Dietary fats are good to use
to add flavor to the meal.

You can find recipe ideas on our website.

7

5. End on a satiating note
Because your appetite is sensory-specific, consuming a new flavor at the end of a meal
can partially reawaken your appetite. Research has found that consuming fruit before a
meal is more effective for satiety than consuming it afterwards, for example. As a
general rule, try to ensure you can satiate the last flavor you consume.

If you do insist on a dessert with a different flavor, make sure to implement the
carbonated water (or perhaps soda) trick to prevent that 1 slice of cake from turning into
1 cake.

8

6. Optimize your cutlery
Your brain uses many cues other than energy content to estimate how much food is
'enough' for you. These includes environmental references of the portion size. We can
use these to trick the brain into believing you're eating more food than you are by eating
with small forks, spoons and knives and serving your meal on plates that are only just
large enough to contain the meal. Also, serve your food on brightly colored plates that
contrast strongly with your food. The result is that your brain perceives your meal as
larger due to the Ebbinghaus-Titchener size-contrast illusion and the Delboeuf effect. As
a result, your brain signals more satiety and you eat less.

9

7. Limit your serving sizes
People eat what they're served. This is called the clean-plate-effect. So only serve
yourself as much as you think you need, not as much as is available.

10

8. Eliminate food cues
Your brain uses food cues, signs of the presence of food in the environment, to
determine how much food is available. Based on that, it stimulates your appetite. This is
why bakers use aromas to give the strong scent of bread that attracts by-walkers. To
avoid your appetite being stimulated by such cues, don't have any food lying on your
kitchen counter and certainly not on your work desk. All your food should be stored
inside the kitchen cabinets.

11

9. Eat at fixed times; don't snack
A schedule of regular eating times entrains your biorhythm to only evoke hunger at the
times you're accustomed to eating. If you sometimes eat breakfast but not always, you'll
be hungry when you don't, but if you never eat breakfast, you get used to it. Concretely,
it's best to always eat at the same time of day: a 2-hour window is recommended for all
meal times.
headings: 9. Eat at fixed times; don’t snack

pages.text:
12

10. Get enough sleep
Sleep deprivation is bad for everything and your appetite is no exception. Not getting at
least 7.5 hours of high quality sleep per night majorly stimulates your appetite.

13

11. Eat mindfully
Your mother probably taught you not to talk with your mouth full. That's good advice, as
distractions enormously increase how much food you eat. The brain has to actively
register food intake to create satiety signals. When the brain is occupied by a TV show,
a laptop screen or the person on the other side of the table, you're consuming energy
without much appetite suppression. Not to mention, you're not enjoying your food then,
which entirely defeats the purpose of eating.

When you eat, focus on your food. Savor it. Don't eat in front of a TV or a computer. If
you want to talk with somebody, first finish your bite and put your cutlery down.

14

12. Chewing gum
One of the signals your brain uses to register food intake is the act of chewing. Science
has provided us with a marvelous invention to take advantage of this system and
suppress our appetite: chewing gum. Chewing gum can significantly decrease feelings
of hunger and reduce how much you eat.

Also, be sure to properly chew your food. This should occur automatically if you're
eating mindfully.

15

13. Keep meals high in carbs or fat but not both
When a meal is rich in carbs and fats, it is typically very easy to overeat on compared to
meals high in only one or the other and protein. You don't have to completely separate
carbs and fats, but it can help not to combine high-carb and high-fat foods in the same
meal. For example, you may not overeat on avocado and you may not overeat on rice
wraps, but if you put avocado in the rice wraps, you get a texture that's easy to overeat
on.
headings: 13. Keep meals high in carbs or fat but not both

all_headings: 9. Eat at fixed times; don’t snack
13. Keep meals high in carbs or fat but not both
